name: Brahmanda Purana
shortDescription: A major Hindu Purana detailing cosmogony, genealogy, and various aspects of dharma.
description: The Brahmanda Purana is one of the eighteen major Puranas, a genre of encyclopedic Hindu texts. It is characterized by its vast scope, encompassing a wide range of topics including cosmogony (the origin of the universe), genealogy of gods, kings, and sages, discussions on dharma (righteous conduct), geography, astronomy, and various philosophical and practical aspects of Hindu life. The text is notable for containing the Lalita Sahasranama, a hymn dedicated to the goddess Lalita Tripura Sundari. The Brahmanda Purana's influence extends to various Hindu traditions, providing a rich source of mythological narratives and religious practices. Its compilation is attributed to the sage Vyasa, though the existing text has likely undergone revisions and additions over centuries. The Purana serves as a valuable resource for understanding Hindu cosmology, social structures, and religious beliefs.
subtitle: The Egg of Brahma: Universe Unveiled
author: Attributed to Vyasa
significance: Provides detailed cosmology and the Lalita Sahasranama.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: The Brahmanda Purana's name, meaning 'Egg of Brahma,' refers to the cosmic egg (Brahmanda) from which the universe emerged. The Purana narrates the creation of the universe by Brahma, the evolution of gods, demons, humans, and other beings. It includes numerous mythological stories about various deities, sages, and kings, illustrating the complex relationships and events that shape the cosmic order. The text also contains accounts of different kalpas (cosmic cycles) and their respective creations and destructions, reflecting the cyclical nature of time in Hindu cosmology. The inclusion of the Lalita Mahatmya and Lalita Sahasranama, which glorify the goddess Lalita Tripura Sundari, highlights the importance of Devi worship within this Purana.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: The Brahmanda Purana plays a significant role in shaping Hindu religious and cultural practices. It provides a comprehensive framework for understanding Hindu cosmology, social structures, and religious beliefs. The text serves as a valuable resource for scholars and practitioners alike, offering insights into various aspects of Hindu philosophy, ritual, and mythology. The Purana's emphasis on dharma (righteous conduct) guides individuals towards ethical living and spiritual growth. The inclusion of hymns and prayers dedicated to various deities, such as the Lalita Sahasranama, encourages devotion and worship. Furthermore, the Brahmanda Purana's narratives and teachings have influenced various art forms, literature, and performing arts throughout India.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: The Brahmanda Purana is rich in symbolism, reflecting the complex and multifaceted nature of Hindu philosophy. The cosmic egg (Brahmanda) represents the potential for creation and the interconnectedness of all things. The genealogies of gods, kings, and sages symbolize the transmission of knowledge and the continuity of tradition. The various deities and their attributes embody different aspects of the divine and the powers of nature. The narratives of creation and destruction represent the cyclical nature of time and the constant process of change. The concept of dharma symbolizes the importance of ethical conduct and the pursuit of spiritual liberation. The Lalita Sahasranama, with its thousand names of the goddess, symbolizes the infinite aspects of the divine feminine.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: The Brahmanda Purana continues to exert a significant influence on contemporary Hindu society. Its narratives and teachings are still widely circulated and studied, informing religious practices, philosophical discussions, and artistic expressions. The Lalita Sahasranama, in particular, remains a popular hymn recited by devotees of the goddess. The Purana's emphasis on dharma continues to guide ethical conduct and social responsibility. Modern scholars continue to analyze and interpret the Brahmanda Purana, shedding new light on its historical context, philosophical significance, and cultural impact. The Purana's enduring legacy reflects its profound contribution to the understanding and practice of Hinduism.

sources.author: G.V. Tagare
sources.description: A comprehensive English translation of the Brahmanda Purana.

sources.author: Wendy Doniger
sources.description: Provides context on the Puranas within the broader Hindu tradition.

associations.name: Egg
associations.significance: Represents the cosmic egg from which the universe emerged.

associations.name: Dharma
associations.significance: Emphasis on righteous conduct and ethical living.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Represents the causal waters before creation.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Hesiod's Theogony
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both texts provide accounts of the origin of the universe and the genealogies of gods.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Enuma Elish
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both narratives describe the creation of the world from primordial chaos.

companions.name: Vyasa
companions.description: Traditional compiler of the Puranas, including the Brahmanda Purana.

companions.name: Brahma
companions.description: The creator god whose cosmic egg (Brahmanda) is the namesake of the Purana.

companions.name: Lalita Tripura Sundari
companions.description: The goddess glorified in the Lalita Sahasranama, a significant part of the Brahmanda Purana.

worshipPractices.name: Lalita Sahasranama Recitation
worshipPractices.description: Devotees recite the thousand names of the goddess Lalita Tripura Sundari as a form of worship.

festivals.name: Navaratri
festivals.description: The nine-night festival dedicated to the goddess Durga, during which hymns from the Brahmanda Purana may be recited.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Brahmanda Purana continues to influence Hindu religious practices, philosophical discussions, and artistic expressions. The Lalita Sahasranama is still widely recited, and the Purana's narratives inform various aspects of Hindu culture.
